what: Bayesian Vector Autoregression Models with Steady-State Priors via Stan
title: Bayesian Vector Autoregression Models with Steady-State Priors via Stan
text: package, user can estimate steady-state BVAR(p) model (Villani, 2009). estimation, user can produce forecasts (unconditional conditional) impulse response functions (orthogonalized generalized). goal SteadyStateBVAR use modern Bayesian tools (Stan) : ) estimate model specified original paper, ii) extend model different ways. Previously, extensions model seemed limited Mattias Villani time derive. See example Clark (2011), extends steady-state BVAR model include Random Walk stochastic volatility: “methodological sense, paper extends estimator Villani (2009) include stochastic volatility.” Later , writes: “(Special thanks due Mattias Villani providing formulas posterior means variances \(\Pi\) \(\Psi\), generalize constant-variance formulas Villani 2009.)” Another example Dieppe, Legrand, van Roye (2016): “Villani (2009) provides derivation case normal-diffuse prior distribution, incoming analysis restricted case.” Times different now, help Stan, essentially limited imagination. time writing, package provides three versions steady-state BVAR model: ) homoscedastic model, .e. original model (Villani, 2009), ii) Random Walk stochastic volatility model (Clark, 2011), iii) AR(1) stochastic volatility model.

title: Bayesian Vector Autoregression Models with Steady-State Priors via Stan
text: can install SteadyStateBVAR : can also install development version SteadyStateBVAR GitHub :
code: install.packages("SteadyStateBVAR") # install.packages("pak") pak::pak("markjwbecker/SteadyStateBVAR")

title: Bayesian Vector Autoregression Models with Steady-State Priors via Stan
text: steady-state BVAR(\(p\)) model (Villani, 2009) \[y_t = \Psi d_t + \Pi_1(y_{t-1}-\Psi d_{t-1})+\dots+\Pi_p(y_{t-p}-\Psi d_{t-p})+u_t\] \(y_t\) \(k\)-dimensional vector endogenous variables (time series) time \(t\), \(d_t\) \(q\)-dimensional vector deterministic (exogenous) variables time \(t\), (reduced-form) innovations \(u_t \sim N_k(0,\Sigma_u)\) independence time periods. \(\Pi_\ell\) \(\ell=1,\dots,p\) \((k \times k)\) matrix, \(\Psi\) \((k \times q)\) matrix. Now \[\mathrm{E}(y_t)=\mu_t=\Psi d_t\] unconditional mean, steady state, process. Since long-horizon forecasts stationary VARs converge unconditional mean (steady state), naturally important forecasting perspective obtain precise inference \(\Psi\). Note current version package allows \(d_t\) contain either constant, constant dummy variable, constant time trend. can stack (transposed) \(\Pi_i\) matrices \((kp \times k)\) matrix \(\beta\) \[\beta= \begin{bmatrix} \Pi'_1 \\ \vdots  \\ \Pi'_p \end{bmatrix}\] can rewrite model nonlinear regression (Karlsson, 2013) \[y_t' =d_t'\Psi' + \left[w_t'-q_t'(I_p \otimes \Psi') \right]\beta +u_t'\] \(w_t'=(y_{t-1}',\dots,y_{t-p}')\) \(kp\)-dimensional vector lagged endogenous variables \(q_t'=(d_{t-1}',\dots,d_{t-p}')\) \(qp\)-dimensional vector lagged deterministic (exogenous) variables, \(I_p\) \((p \times p)\) identity matrix \(\otimes\) denotes Kronecker product. likelihood written Stan code. goal estimate \(\beta, \Psi\), \(\Sigma_u\), priors needed. First, prior independence \(\beta, \Psi\) \(\Sigma_u\) assumed. Starting \(\beta\), use Minnesota prior \[\mathrm{vec}(\beta) \sim \mathrm{N}_{kpk} \left[\theta_\beta,\Omega_\beta\right]\] prior means (elements \(\theta_\beta\)) set \[\begin{aligned} \mathrm{E}\left(\Pi_{\ell}^{(,j)}\right)&= \begin{cases} \kappa & \text{} \ell = 1 \ \mathrm{} \ = j \\ 0 & \mathrm{otherwise} \end{cases}\\ \kappa&= \begin{cases} \kappa^{level} & \text{} \mathrm{variable} \ \ \mathrm{level} \\ \kappa^{\Delta} & \text{}\mathrm{variable} \ \ \mathrm{difference} \end{cases}\\ \end{aligned}\] , autoregressive coefficient \(\Pi_{\ell}^{(,j)}\) element \(\left(,j\right)\) \(\Pi_{\ell}\) \(\ell=1,\dots,p\). , Minnesota prior sets prior means elements \(\beta\) \(0\), except elements relate first lags variables, set \(\kappa\). variable \(\) level (e.g. nominal interest rate), \(\kappa=\kappa^{level}\), typical choices \(\kappa^{level}\) \(1\) \(0.9\). Evaluating equations prior means, equation \(\) becomes random walk \(\kappa^{level}=1\) persistent stationary AR(1) process \(\kappa^{level}=0.9\). Since steady state exists process stationary, \(0.9\) recommended steady-state BVAR. variable \(\) difference (e.g. output growth), \(\kappa=\kappa^{\Delta}\), common choice \(\kappa^{\Delta}\) \(0\), .e. equation \(\) becomes (evaluating prior means) random walk expressed first differences. differenced variable still shows degree persistence (can examined ACF plot), suitable value \(\kappa^{\Delta}\) can (example) \(0.6\) instead \(0\). Moving prior variances, \(\Omega_\beta\) diagonal matrix containing prior variances elements \(\beta\). specified \[\mathrm{Var}\left(\Pi_{\ell}^{(,j)}\right)= \begin{cases} \left(\frac{\lambda_1}{\ell^{\lambda_3}}\right)^2 & \text{} = j \\ \left(\frac{\lambda_1 \lambda_2\sigma_i}{\ell^{\lambda_3}\sigma_j}\right)^2& \text{} \neq j \end{cases}\] \(\lambda_1\), \(\lambda_2\), \(\lambda_3\) scalar hyperparameters known overall tightness, cross-equation tightness lag decay rate. Furthermore, \(\sigma_i^2\) \((,)\):th element \(\Sigma_u\), know therefore replace estimate. package, replaced least squares residual variance univariate autoregression variable \(\) \(p\) lags (including constant dummy/trend variable applicable). Moving \(\Psi\), prior use \[\mathrm{vec}(\Psi) \sim \mathrm{N}_{kq}\left[\theta_\Psi,\Omega_\Psi\right]\] really core steady-state BVAR model. \(\theta_\Psi\), specify prior beliefs location steady state, \(\Omega_\Psi\), assume diagonal matrix, specify degree certainty prior beliefs. Finally, prior \(\Sigma_u\) usual noninformative Jeffreys prior \[p(\Sigma_u) \propto\left|\Sigma_u \right|^{-(k+1)/2}\] However, user wants, inverse Wishart prior can used instead \[\Sigma_u \sim \mathrm{IW}(V_0,m_0)\] \(V_0\) scale matrix \(m_0\geq k+2\) number degrees freedom. uninformative prior can (package) specified setting \(V_0=(m_0-k-1)\hat{\Sigma}_u\) \(\hat{\Sigma}_u\) least squares estimate VAR(\(p\)) (including constant dummy/trend variable applicable), \(m_0=k+2\). package also allows stochastic volatility (Random Walk AR1 specifications), let covariance matrix innovations vary time (.e. \(\Sigma_{u,t}\), details found ?bvar).

title: Bayesian Vector Autoregression Models with Steady-State Priors via Stan
text: estimate model Section 4.1 Villani (2009), produce (un)conditional forecasts perform impulse response analysis, simply run following code:
code: library(SteadyStateBVAR) data("Villani2009") yt <- Villani2009  #hold out last two observations to facilitate comparisons to Figures 1-3 in Villani (2009) yt <- ts(yt[1:102, ], start = start(yt), frequency = frequency(yt))  bvar_obj <- bvar(data = yt)  # Use a dummy to model Sweden’s change in monetary policy in the 1990s # (move to inflation targeting and flexible exchange rate) bp <- which(time(yt) == 1992.75) #breakpoint dum_var <- c(rep(1,bp), rep(0,nrow(yt)-bp))  bvar_obj <- setup(bvar_obj,                   p=4,                   deterministic = "constant_and_dummy",                   dummy = dum_var)  lambda_1 <- 0.2 #overall tightness lambda_2 <- 0.5 #cross-equation tightness lambda_3 <- 1.0 #lag decay rate  #fol_pm = first own lag prior means fol_pm=c(0,   #delta y_f          0,   #pi_f          0.9, #i_f          0,   #delta y          0,   #pi          0.9, #i          0.9  #q          )  #psi_1 = Psi col 1 #psi_2 = Psi col 2  #95% prior probability intervals (normal distribution) #See Table I in Villani (2009) theta_Psi <-    c(   ppi( 2.00,  3.00,  annualized_growthrate=TRUE)$mean,   #psi_1: delta y_f   ppi( 1.50,  2.50,  annualized_growthrate=TRUE)$mean,   #psi_1: pi_f   ppi( 4.50,  5.50,  annualized_growthrate=FALSE)$mean,  #psi_1: i_f   ppi( 2.00,  2.50,  annualized_growthrate=TRUE)$mean,   #psi_1: delta y   ppi( 1.70,  2.30,  annualized_growthrate=TRUE)$mean,   #psi_1: pi   ppi( 4.00,  4.50,  annualized_growthrate=FALSE)$mean,  #psi_1: i   ppi( 3.85,  4.00,  annualized_growthrate=FALSE)$mean,  #psi_1: q   ppi(-1.00,  1.00,  annualized_growthrate=TRUE)$mean,   #psi_2: delta y_f   ppi( 1.50,  2.50,  annualized_growthrate=TRUE)$mean,   #psi_2: pi_f   ppi( 1.50,  2.50,  annualized_growthrate=FALSE)$mean,  #psi_2: i_f   ppi(-1.00,  1.00,  annualized_growthrate=TRUE)$mean,   #psi_2: delta y   ppi( 4.30,  5.70,  annualized_growthrate=TRUE)$mean,   #psi_2: pi   ppi( 3.00,  5.50,  annualized_growthrate=FALSE)$mean,  #psi_2: i   ppi(-0.50,  0.50,  annualized_growthrate=FALSE)$mean   #psi_2: q   )  Omega_Psi <-    diag(   c(   ppi( 2.00,  3.00,  annualized_growthrate=TRUE)$var,    #psi_1: delta y_f   ppi( 1.50,  2.50,  annualized_growthrate=TRUE)$var,    #psi_1: pi_f   ppi( 4.50,  5.50,  annualized_growthrate=FALSE)$var,   #psi_1: i_f   ppi( 2.00,  2.50,  annualized_growthrate=TRUE)$var,    #psi_1: delta y   ppi( 1.70,  2.30,  annualized_growthrate=TRUE)$var,    #psi_1: pi   ppi( 4.00,  4.50,  annualized_growthrate=FALSE)$var,   #psi_1: i   ppi( 3.85,  4.00,  annualized_growthrate=FALSE)$var,   #psi_1: q   ppi(-1.00,  1.00,  annualized_growthrate=TRUE)$var,    #psi_2: delta y_f   ppi( 1.50,  2.50,  annualized_growthrate=TRUE)$var,    #psi_2: pi_f   ppi( 1.50,  2.50,  annualized_growthrate=FALSE)$var,   #psi_2: i_f   ppi(-1.00,  1.00,  annualized_growthrate=TRUE)$var,    #psi_2: delta y   ppi( 4.30,  5.70,  annualized_growthrate=TRUE)$var,    #psi_2: pi   ppi( 3.00,  5.50,  annualized_growthrate=FALSE)$var,   #psi_2: i   ppi(-0.50,  0.50,  annualized_growthrate=FALSE)$var    #psi_2: q   )   )  bvar_obj <- priors(bvar_obj,                    lambda_1,                    lambda_2,                    lambda_3,                    fol_pm,                    theta_Psi,                    Omega_Psi,                    Jeffrey=TRUE)  p <- bvar_obj$setup$p k <- bvar_obj$setup$k kf <- 3 #foreign variables  restriction_matrix <- matrix(1, k*p, k)  for(i in 1:p){   rows <- ((i-1)*k + kf + 1) : (i*k)   cols <- 1:kf   restriction_matrix[rows, cols] <- 0 }  #block exogeneity for foreign variables bvar_obj <- restrict_beta(bvar_obj, restriction_matrix)  #fit the model bvar_obj <- fit(bvar_obj,                 H = 12,                 d_pred = cbind(rep(1, 12), 0),                 iter = 10000,                 warmup = 2500,                 chains = 2,                 cores = 2)  #posterior summaries summary(bvar_obj , stat = "mean")  #unconditional forecasts #see last forecasts in Figures 1-3 in Villani (2009) fcst <- forecast(bvar_obj,                  ci = 0.95,                  fcst_type = "mean",                  growth_rate_idx = c(4,5), #annual inflation/real GDP growth instead of QoQ                  plot_idx = c(4,5,6))  #conditional forecasts conditions <- data.frame(               var     = rep(6,12),               horizon = rep(1:12),               value   = seq(2, 8, length.out = 12)) #toy scenario                cond_fcst <- conditional_forecast(bvar_obj,                     conditions,                     ci=0.68,                     fcst_type = "mean",                     plot_idx = c(4,6),                     growth_rate_idx = c(4))  irf <- IRF(bvar_obj,            H=20,            response=5,#inflation            shock=6, #interest rate            type="median",            method="OIRF",            ci=0.68,            growth_rate_idx=5) #annual inflation instead of QoQ

what: Impulse Response Functions for a fitted steady-state BVAR model — IRF
title: Impulse Response Functions for a fitted steady-state BVAR model — IRF
text: Computes plots impulse response functions (IRFs) fitted steady-state bvar object. Supports orthogonalized (OIRF) generalized (GIRF) impulse responses, optional conversion annual growth rates.

title: Impulse Response Functions for a fitted steady-state BVAR model — IRF
code: IRF(   x,   H = 16,   response = NULL,   shock = NULL,   type = c("median", "mean"),   method = c("OIRF", "GIRF"),   ci = 0.95,   t = NULL,   growth_rate_idx = NULL )

title: Impulse Response Functions for a fitted steady-state BVAR model — IRF
text: x steady-state bvar object passed fit. H Integer. forecast horizon IRF. Default 16. response Integer. Index response variable plot. NULL (default), responses plotted. shock Integer. Index shock variable plot. NULL (default), shocks plotted. type Character. Whether use "median" "mean" point estimate. Default "median". method Character. IRF method: "OIRF" orthogonalized "GIRF" generalized impulse responses. Default "OIRF". ci Numeric. credible interval width. Default 0.95, .e. 95%. t Integer. Time index covariance matrix using stochastic volatility models. NULL (default), last time t used. growth_rate_idx Integer vector. Indices variables convert annual growth rates. Default NULL.

title: Impulse Response Functions for a fitted steady-state BVAR model — IRF
text: Invisibly returns list three arrays: point estimate IRF, lower, upper credible bounds, dimension k x k x (H+1).

title: Impulse Response Functions for a fitted steady-state BVAR model — IRF
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ #homoscedastic with Jeffreys prior yt <- matrix(rnorm(50), 25, 2)  bvar_obj <- bvar(data = yt)  bvar_obj <- setup(bvar_obj, p=1, deterministic = "constant")  bvar_obj <- priors(bvar_obj,                    lambda_1 = 0.2,                    lambda_2 = 0.5,                    lambda_3 = 1,                    first_own_lag_prior_mean = rep(1,2),                    theta_Psi = rep(0, 2),                    Omega_Psi = diag(0.1, 2, 2),                    Jeffrey = TRUE,                    SV = FALSE,                    SV_type = NULL,                    SV_priors = NULL)                     bvar_obj <- fit(bvar_obj,                 H = 8,                 d_pred = matrix(rep(1,8)),                 iter = 200,                 warmup = 50,                 chains = 1,                 cores = 1,                 verbose = FALSE,                 auto_write = FALSE)                  IRF(bvar_obj) } # }

what: SteadyStateBVAR: Bayesian Vector Autoregression Models with Steady-State Priors via Stan — SteadyStateBVAR-package
title: SteadyStateBVAR: Bayesian Vector Autoregression Models with Steady-State Priors via Stan — SteadyStateBVAR-package
text: Provides estimation Bayesian Vector Autoregression ('BVAR') models steady-state priors via 'Stan'. estimation, package provides functions unconditional conditional forecasting, impulse response analysis. details steady-state BVAR model see Villani (2009) doi:10.1002/jae.1065 .

what: Create a steady-state BVAR model object — bvar
title: Create a steady-state BVAR model object — bvar
text: Initialises steady-state bvar object. starting point models SteadyStateBVAR. creation, pass object sequentially setup, priors, fit build estimate model.

title: Create a steady-state BVAR model object — bvar
code: bvar(data)

title: Create a steady-state BVAR model object — bvar
text: data numeric matrix time series data column variable row time period.

title: Create a steady-state BVAR model object — bvar
text: object class bvar.

title: Create a steady-state BVAR model object — bvar
text:
model takes form $$y_t = \Psi d_t + \Pi_1(y_{t-1}-\Psi d_{t-1})+\dots+\Pi_p(y_{t-p}-\Psi d_{t-p})+u_t$$ \(y_t\) \(k\)-dimensional vector endogenous variables time \(t\), \(d_t\) \(q\)-dimensional vector deterministic (exogenous) variables time \(t\). \(\Pi_\ell\) \(\ell=1,\dots,p\) \((k 	imes k)\) matrix autoregressive parameters, \(\Psi\) \((k 	imes q)\) matrix steady-state parameters. Note \(\mathrm{E}(y_t)=\mu_t=\Psi d_t\) unconditional mean, steady state process. One can stack (transposed) \(\Pi_i\) matrices \((kp 	imes k)\) matrix \( eta\) $$ eta= egin{bmatrix}\Pi'_1 \ \vdots  \\Pi'_p\end{bmatrix}$$ model can rewritten nonlinear regression (Karlsson, 2013) $$y_t' =d_t'\Psi' + \left[w_t'-q_t'(I_p \otimes \Psi') ight] eta +u_t'$$ \(w_t'=(y_{t-1}',\dots,y_{t-p}')\) \(kp\)-dimensional vector lagged endogenous variables \(q_t'=(d_{t-1}',\dots,d_{t-p}')\) \(qp\)-dimensional vector lagged deterministic (exogenous) variables, \(I_p\) \((p 	imes p)\) identity matrix \(\otimes\) denotes Kronecker product. likelihood written Stan code. goal estimate \( eta, \Psi\), \(\Sigma_u\). innovations model, case homoscedastic steady-state BVAR, \(u_t \sim \mathrm{N_k}(0,\Sigma_u)\). However, models stochastic volatility, instead time-varying innovation covariance matrix \(u_t \sim \mathrm{N_k}(0,\Sigma_{u,t})\). innovations take form $$ egin{aligned} u_t &= ^{-1} \Lambda^{0.5}_t \epsilon_t \ \epsilon_t &\sim \mathrm{N}(0, \mathrm{}_k)\end{aligned}$$ \(\) lower triangular matrix ones diagonal describes contemporaneous interaction endogenous variables, $$\Lambda_t = \mathrm{diag}(\lambda_{1,t},\dots,\lambda_{k,t})$$ contains time-varying variances (log volatilities) conditionally Gaussian shocks. AR1 stochastic volatility specification, log volatilities follow AR(1) processes $$\ln \lambda_{,t} = \gamma_{0,} + \gamma_{1,} \ln \lambda_{,t-1} + 
u_{,t}, \ =1,\dots,k$$ log volatility AR(1) processes restricted stationary region, .e. \(|\gamma_{1,}|<1 \  orall \). RW stochastic volatility specification, log volatilities follow Random Walk processes $$\gamma_{0,}=0, \ \gamma_{1,}=1 \  orall $$ innovations log volatilities follow AR1 case $$
u_{t} = (
u_{1,t},\dots,
u_{k,t})'\sim \mathrm{N}(0, \Phi)$$ \(\Phi\) diagonal innovations log volatilities allowed correlated across variables. RW case, \(\Phi\) diagonal variances \(\phi_i\) \(=1,\dots,k\). Note stochastic volatility specifications, time-varying covariance matrix $$\Sigma_{u,t} = ^{-1} \Lambda_t (^{-1})'$$ details homoscedastic steady-state BVAR model, see Villani (2009). Random Walk stochastic volatility steady-state BVAR model, see Clark (2011). details regarding AR(1) stochastic volatility, see Carriero, Clark Marcellino (2024).

title: Create a steady-state BVAR model object — bvar
text: Carriero, ., Clark, T. E., Marcellino, M. (2024). Capturing Macro‐Economic Tail Risks Bayesian Vector Journal Money, Credit Banking. 56(5), pp. 1099–1127. Clark, T. E. (2011). Real-Time Density Forecasts Bayesian Vector Autoregressions Stochastic Volatility. Journal Business & Economic Statistics. 29(3), pp. 327–341. Karlsson, S. (2013). Forecasting Bayesian Vector Autoregression. : Elliott, G. Timmerman, . (eds) Handbook Economic Forecasting. Elsevier B.V. Vol 2, Part B., pp. 791-897. Villani, M. (2009). Steady-state priors vector autoregressions. Journal Applied Econometrics. 24(4), pp. 630–649.

title: Create a steady-state BVAR model object — bvar
code: yt <- matrix(rnorm(50), 25, 2)  bvar_obj <- bvar(data = yt)

what: Conditional forecasts from a fitted BVAR model — conditional_forecast
title: Conditional forecasts from a fitted BVAR model — conditional_forecast
text: Computes plots conditional forecasts fitted steady-state bvar object. Conditions imposed specific variables specific horizons using method Dieppe, Legrand, van Roye (2016). conditional unconditional forecasts plotted comparison. Please note moment, conditional forecasting enabled homoscedastic steady-state BVAR, .e. SV=FALSE priors().

title: Conditional forecasts from a fitted BVAR model — conditional_forecast
code: conditional_forecast(   bvar_obj,   conditions,   ci = 0.95,   fcst_type = c("mean", "median"),   growth_rate_idx = NULL,   plot_idx = NULL )

title: Conditional forecasts from a fitted BVAR model — conditional_forecast
text: bvar_obj steady-state bvar object passed fit. conditions data frame three columns: var (integer index conditioned variable), horizon (integer forecast horizon condition imposed), value (numeric, imposed condition). Note var horizon must integers, used indexing. ci Numeric. prediction interval width. Default 0.95. fcst_type Character. Whether use "mean" "median" point forecast. Default "mean". growth_rate_idx Integer vector. Indices variables convert annual growth rates. Default NULL. plot_idx Integer vector. Indices variables plot. NULL (default), variables plotted.

title: Conditional forecasts from a fitted BVAR model — conditional_forecast
text: Invisibly returns list three matrices: forecast, lower, upper, dimension H x k, well cond_draws, array posterior conditional forecast draws.

title: Conditional forecasts from a fitted BVAR model — conditional_forecast
text: Dieppe, ., van Roye, B., Legrand, R. (2016). BEAR toolbox. Working Paper Series, . 1934. European Central Bank.

title: Conditional forecasts from a fitted BVAR model — conditional_forecast
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ #homoscedastic with Jeffreys prior yt <- matrix(rnorm(50), 25, 2)  bvar_obj <- bvar(data = yt)  bvar_obj <- setup(bvar_obj, p=1, deterministic = "constant")  bvar_obj <- priors(bvar_obj,                    lambda_1 = 0.2,                    lambda_2 = 0.5,                    lambda_3 = 1,                    first_own_lag_prior_mean = rep(1,2),                    theta_Psi = rep(0, 2),                    Omega_Psi = diag(0.1, 2, 2),                    Jeffrey = TRUE,                    SV = FALSE,                    SV_type = NULL,                    SV_priors = NULL)                     bvar_obj <- fit(bvar_obj,                 H = 8,                 d_pred = matrix(rep(1,8)),                 iter = 200,                 warmup = 50,                 chains = 1,                 cores = 1,                 verbose = FALSE,                 auto_write = FALSE)                  conditions <- data.frame(var = rep(2,8),                          horizon = rep(1:8),                          value   = rep(1,8))                           cond_fcst <- conditional_forecast(bvar_obj,                                   conditions,                                   ci=0.68,                                   fcst_type = "mean") } # }

what: Estimate the steady-state BVAR model using Stan — fit
title: Estimate the steady-state BVAR model using Stan — fit
text: Estimates steady-state BVAR model using  -U-Turn sampler (variant Hamiltonian Monte Carlo) via Stan. Uses data, setup, priors stored steady-state bvar object. Supports homoscedastic stochastic volatility (RW AR1) specifications.

title: Estimate the steady-state BVAR model using Stan — fit
code: fit(   x,   H = 1,   d_pred = NULL,   iter = 2000,   warmup = floor(iter/2),   chains = 1,   cores = getOption("mc.cores", 1L),   verbose = FALSE,   auto_write = FALSE )

title: Estimate the steady-state BVAR model using Stan — fit
text: x steady-state bvar object passed setup priors. H Positive Integer. Forecast horizon. Default 1. d_pred Matrix size H x q. Future values deterministic variables d_t. Default NULL, must provided user. iter Integer. Total number MCMC iterations per chain. Default 2000. warmup Integer. Number warmup (burn-) iterations per chain. Default floor(iter/2). chains Integer. Number MCMC chains. Default 1. cores Positive integer specifying number CPU cores used sampling. Default getOption("mc.cores", 1L), .e. mc.cores option (set), otherwise defaults 1 core. verbose Logical indicating whether print intermediate output Stan console, defaults FALSE. auto_write Logical indicating whether Stan models automatically written disk cache via rstan. Default FALSE.

title: Estimate the steady-state BVAR model using Stan — fit
text: fitted steady-state bvar object : x$fit$stan: object class stanfit x$fit$posterior_means: List posterior mean estimates x$fit$posterior_medians: List posterior median estimates

title: Estimate the steady-state BVAR model using Stan — fit
text: function selects appropriate Stan model based prior settings: Homoscedastic Jeffreys prior: steady_state_bvar_homoscedastic_jeffreys_prior.stan Homoscedastic uninformative inverse-Wishart prior: steady_state_bvar_homoscedastic_inverse_wishart_prior.stan Random Walk stochastic volatility: steady_state_bvar_RW_stochastic_volatility.stan AR1 stochastic volatility: steady_state_bvar_AR1_stochastic_volatility.stan function estimates following parameters (see bvar details): beta: kp×k VAR coefficient matrix Psi: k×q steady-state parameter matrix Sigma_u: innovation covariance matrix (k×k homoscedastic, T×k×k stochastic volatility) Random Walk stochastic volatility: : k×k lower triangular matrix ones diagonal describes contemporaneous interaction endogenous variables phi: k-dimensional vector log volatility innovation variances AR1 stochastic volatility: : k×k lower triangular matrix ones diagonal describes contemporaneous interaction endogenous variables gamma_0: k-dimensional vector log volatility intercepts gamma_1: k-dimensional vector log volatility slopes Phi: k×k log volatility innovation covariance matrix

title: Estimate the steady-state BVAR model using Stan — fit
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ #homoscedastic with Jeffreys prior yt <- matrix(rnorm(50), 25, 2)  bvar_obj <- bvar(data = yt)  bvar_obj <- setup(bvar_obj, p=1, deterministic = "constant")  bvar_obj <- priors(bvar_obj,                    lambda_1 = 0.2,                    lambda_2 = 0.5,                    lambda_3 = 1,                    first_own_lag_prior_mean = rep(1,2),                    theta_Psi = rep(0, 2),                    Omega_Psi = diag(0.1, 2, 2),                    Jeffrey = TRUE,                    SV = FALSE,                    SV_type = NULL,                    SV_priors = NULL)                     bvar_obj <- fit(bvar_obj,                 H = 8,                 d_pred = matrix(rep(1,8)),                 iter = 200,                 warmup = 50,                 chains = 1,                 cores = 1,                 verbose = FALSE,                 auto_write = FALSE)                     #RW stochastic volatility yt <- matrix(rnorm(50), 25, 2)  bvar_obj <- bvar(data = yt)  bvar_obj <- setup(bvar_obj, p=1, deterministic = "constant")  k <- bvar_obj$setup$k n_free_params_A <- bvar_obj$setup$n_free_params_A  SV_priors_RW <- list( theta_A             =  rep(0, n_free_params_A), Omega_A             =  diag(1000, n_free_params_A), mu_log_lambda_0     =  rep(0, k), sigma2_log_lambda_0 =  rep(1000, k), alpha_phi           =  rep(5, k), beta_phi            = (rep(5, k) - 1) * rep(0.1, k) )  bvar_obj <- priors(bvar_obj,                    lambda_1 = 0.2,                    lambda_2 = 0.5,                    lambda_3 = 1,                    first_own_lag_prior_mean = rep(1,2),                    theta_Psi = rep(0, 2),                    Omega_Psi = diag(0.1, 2, 2),                    SV = TRUE,                    SV_type = "RW",                    SV_priors = SV_priors_RW)  bvar_obj <- fit(bvar_obj,                 H = 8,                 d_pred = matrix(rep(1,8)),                 iter = 200,                 warmup = 50,                 chains = 1,                 cores = 1,                 verbose = FALSE,                 auto_write = FALSE)                     #AR1 stochastic volatility yt <- matrix(rnorm(50), 25, 2)  bvar_obj <- bvar(data = yt)  bvar_obj <- setup(bvar_obj, p=1, deterministic = "constant")  k <- bvar_obj$setup$k n_free_params_A <- bvar_obj$setup$n_free_params_A  SV_priors_AR <- list( theta_A            =  rep(0, n_free_params_A), Omega_A            =  diag(1000, n_free_params_A), theta_gamma_0      =  rep(0.1, k), Omega_gamma_0      =  diag(1000, k), theta_gamma_1      =  rep(0.9, k), Omega_gamma_1      =  diag(10, k), theta_log_lambda_0 =  rep(0.1, k)/(1-rep(0.9, k)), Omega_log_lambda_0 =  diag(1000, k), V_0                = (10 - k - 1) * diag(k), m_0                =  10 )  bvar_obj <- priors(bvar_obj,                    lambda_1 = 0.2,                    lambda_2 = 0.5,                    lambda_3 = 1,                    first_own_lag_prior_mean = rep(1,2),                    theta_Psi = rep(0, 2),                    Omega_Psi = diag(0.1, 2, 2),                    SV = TRUE,                    SV_type = "AR1",                    SV_priors = SV_priors_AR)   bvar_obj <- fit(bvar_obj,                 H = 8,                 d_pred = matrix(rep(1,8)),                 iter = 200,                 warmup = 50,                 chains = 1,                 cores = 1,                 verbose = FALSE,                 auto_write = FALSE) } # }

what: Forecast from a fitted BVAR model — forecast
title: Forecast from a fitted BVAR model — forecast
text: Computes plots forecasts fitted bvar object. Posterior predictive draws Stan used construct point forecasts prediction intervals. Optionally converts monthly quarterly growth rate forecasts annual growth rates selected variables.

title: Forecast from a fitted BVAR model — forecast
code: forecast(   x,   ci = 0.95,   fcst_type = c("mean", "median"),   growth_rate_idx = NULL,   plot_idx = NULL,   show_all = FALSE )

title: Forecast from a fitted BVAR model — forecast
text: x steady-state bvar object passed fit. ci Numeric. prediction interval width. Default 0.95, .e. 95% prediction interval. fcst_type Character. Whether use "mean" "median" point forecast. Default "mean". growth_rate_idx Integer vector. Indices variables convert annual growth rates. Default NULL. plot_idx Integer vector. Indices variables plot. NULL (default), variables plotted. show_all Logical. FALSE (default), last two years history shown alongside forecast. TRUE, full history shown.

title: Forecast from a fitted BVAR model — forecast
text: Invisibly returns list three matrices: forecast, lower, upper, dimension H x k H forecast horizon k number variables.

title: Forecast from a fitted BVAR model — forecast
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ #homoscedastic with Jeffreys prior yt <- matrix(rnorm(50), 25, 2)  bvar_obj <- bvar(data = yt)  bvar_obj <- setup(bvar_obj, p=1, deterministic = "constant")  bvar_obj <- priors(bvar_obj,                    lambda_1 = 0.2,                    lambda_2 = 0.5,                    lambda_3 = 1,                    first_own_lag_prior_mean = rep(1,2),                    theta_Psi = rep(0, 2),                    Omega_Psi = diag(0.1, 2, 2),                    Jeffrey = TRUE,                    SV = FALSE,                    SV_type = NULL,                    SV_priors = NULL)                     bvar_obj <- fit(bvar_obj,                 H = 8,                 d_pred = matrix(rep(1,8)),                 iter = 200,                 warmup = 50,                 chains = 1,                 cores = 1,                 verbose = FALSE,                 auto_write = FALSE)  forecast(bvar_obj, ci = 0.90, show_all = TRUE) } # }

what: Prior Probability Interval for a Normal Distribution — ppi
title: Prior Probability Interval for a Normal Distribution — ppi
text: Calculates mean variance normal prior probability interval. Given lower upper bound prior probability interval function recovers implied normal prior parameters. Useful specifying informative priors steady-state parameters (elements Psi).

title: Prior Probability Interval for a Normal Distribution — ppi
code: ppi(l, u, interval = 0.95, annualized_growthrate = FALSE, freq = 4)

title: Prior Probability Interval for a Normal Distribution — ppi
text: l Numeric. lower bound prior probability interval. u Numeric. upper bound prior probability interval. interval Numeric. prior probability mass within interval. Default 0.95, .e. 95%. annualized_growthrate Logical. TRUE, converts implied mean variance annualized scale quarterly/monthly etc. scale dividing freq. Default FALSE. freq Integer. data frequency (e.g. 4 quarterly). used annualized_growthrate = TRUE. Default 4.

title: Prior Probability Interval for a Normal Distribution — ppi
text: list two elements: mean var, giving mean variance implied normal distribution.

title: Prior Probability Interval for a Normal Distribution — ppi

what: Specify priors for the steady-state BVAR model — priors
title: Specify priors for the steady-state BVAR model — priors
text: function prepares priors. Minnesota prior used autoregressive parameters, determined overall tightness, cross-equation tightness, lag decay rate. steady-state parameters, normal prior used. covariance matrix innovations, user can choose Jeffreys prior uninformative inverse-Wishart prior. Optionally enables stochastic volatility covariance matrix innovations varies time (random walk AR(1)).

title: Specify priors for the steady-state BVAR model — priors
code: priors(   x,   lambda_1 = 0.2,   lambda_2 = 0.5,   lambda_3 = 1,   first_own_lag_prior_mean = NULL,   theta_Psi = NULL,   Omega_Psi = NULL,   Jeffrey = TRUE,   SV = FALSE,   SV_type = NULL,   SV_priors = NULL )

title: Specify priors for the steady-state BVAR model — priors
text: x steady-state bvar object passed setup. lambda_1 Numeric. Overall tightness Minnesota prior. Default 0.2. lambda_2 Numeric. Cross-equation tightness Minnesota prior. Default 0.5. lambda_3 Numeric. Lag decay rate Minnesota prior. Default 1. first_own_lag_prior_mean Numeric vector length k. Prior means first lags variables. NULL (default), zero vector used. theta_Psi Numeric vector. Prior mean vector vec(Psi), .e. steady-state parameters. NULL (default), OLS estimates used. Omega_Psi Numeric matrix. Prior covariance matrix vec(Psi), .e. steady-state parameters. NULL (default), diagonal matrix variances 1000 used. Jeffrey Logical. TRUE (default), uses Jeffreys prior innovation covariance matrix. FALSE, uses uninformative inverse-Wishart prior. considered SV=FALSE. SV Logical. TRUE, enables stochastic volatility specification. Default FALSE. SV_type Character. Type stochastic volatility model. Must either "RW" "AR1". Required SV = TRUE. SV_priors List. User-supplied stochastic volatility priors. Required SV = TRUE. list must contain following named elements depending SV_type: "RW": theta_A, Omega_A, mu_log_lambda_0, sigma2_log_lambda_0, alpha_phi, beta_phi. "AR1": theta_A, Omega_A, theta_gamma_0, Omega_gamma_0, theta_gamma_1, Omega_gamma_1, theta_log_lambda_0, Omega_log_lambda_0, V_0, m_0.

title: Specify priors for the steady-state BVAR model — priors
text: steady-state bvar object appended priors list containing: theta_beta Prior mean vector vec(beta) constructed Minnesota prior Omega_beta Prior covariance matrix vec(beta) constructed Minnesota prior theta_Psi Prior mean vector vec(Psi), .e. steady-state parameters Omega_Psi Prior covariance matrix vec(Psi), .e. steady-state parameters Jeffrey Indicator Jeffreys prior usage Sigma_AR Residual variance estimates univariate AR fits, used Minnesota prior m_0 Inverse-Wishart prior degrees freedom (Jeffrey = FALSE) V_0 Inverse-Wishart prior scale matrix (Jeffrey = FALSE) SV Logical indicator stochastic volatility specification SV_type Stochastic volatility specification type SV_priors User-supplied SV prior list (SV = TRUE)

title: Specify priors for the steady-state BVAR model — priors
text: goal estimate \(\beta, \Psi\), \(\Sigma_u\), priors needed. First, prior independence \(\beta, \Psi\) \(\Sigma_u\) assumed. Starting \(\beta\), Minnesota prior used $$\mathrm{vec}(\beta) \sim \mathrm{N}_{kpk} \left[\theta_\beta,\Omega_\beta\right]$$ prior means (elements \(\theta_\beta\)) set $$ \begin{aligned} \mathrm{E}\left(\Pi_{\ell}^{(,j)}\right)&= \begin{cases}\kappa & \text{} \ell = 1 \ \text{} \ = j \\0 & \text{otherwise}\end{cases}\\ \kappa&=\begin{cases}\kappa^{level} & \text{} \text{variable} \ \ \text{level} \\ \kappa^{\Delta} & \text{} \text{variable} \ \ \text{difference} \end{cases}\\ \end{aligned} $$ , autoregressive coefficient \(\Pi_{\ell}^{(,j)}\) element \(\left(,j\right)\) \(\Pi_{\ell}\) \(\ell=1,\dots,p\). , Minnesota prior sets prior means elements \(\beta\) \(0\), except elements relate first lags variables, set \(\kappa\). variable \(\) level (e.g. nominal interest rate), \(\kappa=\kappa^{level}\), typical choices \(\kappa^{level}\) \(1\) \(0.9\). Evaluating equations prior means, equation \(\) becomes random walk \(\kappa^{level}=1\) persistent stationary AR(1) process \(\kappa^{level}=0.9\). Since steady state exists process stationary, \(0.9\) recommended steady-state BVAR. variable \(\) difference (e.g. output growth), \(\kappa=\kappa^{\Delta}\), common choice \(\kappa^{\Delta}\) \(0\), .e. equation \(\) becomes (evaluating prior means) random walk expressed first differences. differenced variable still shows degree persistence (can examined ACF plot), suitable value \(\kappa^{\Delta}\) can (example) \(0.6\) instead \(0\). Moving prior variances, \(\Omega_\beta\) diagonal matrix containing prior variances elements \(\beta\). specified $$\mathrm{Var}\left(\Pi_{\ell}^{(,j)}\right)= \begin{cases}\left(\frac{\lambda_1}{\ell^{\lambda_3}}\right)^2 & \text{} = j \\ \left(\frac{\lambda_1 \lambda_2\sigma_i}{\ell^{\lambda_3}\sigma_j}\right)^2& \text{} \neq j \end{cases}$$ \(\lambda_1\), \(\lambda_2\), \(\lambda_3\) scalar hyperparameters known overall tightness, cross-equation tightness lag decay rate. Furthermore, \(\sigma_i^2\) \((,)\):th element \(\Sigma_u\), unknown therefore replaced estimate. package, replaced least squares residual variance univariate autoregression variable \(\) \(p\) lags (including constant dummy/trend variable applicable). Moving \(\Psi\), prior $$\mathrm{vec}(\Psi) \sim \mathrm{N}_{kq}\left[\theta_\Psi,\Omega_\Psi\right]$$ core steady-state BVAR model. \(\theta_\Psi\), one specifies prior beliefs location steady state, \(\Omega_\Psi\), assumed diagonal matrix, one specifies degree certainty prior beliefs. prior \(\Sigma_u\) either usual noninformative Jeffreys prior $$p(\Sigma_u) \propto\left|\Sigma_u \right|^{-(k+1)/2}$$ inverse-Wishart prior $$\Sigma_u \sim \mathrm{IW}(V_0,m_0)$$ \(V_0\) scale matrix \(m_0\geq k+2\) number degrees freedom. uninformative prior can (package) specified setting \(V_0=(m_0-k-1)\hat{\Sigma}_u\) \(\hat{\Sigma}_u\) least squares estimate VAR(\(p\)) (including constant dummy/trend variable applicable), \(m_0=k+2\). stochastic volatility specifications, innovation covariance matrix now time-varying \(\Sigma_{u,t}\). Therefore, stochastic volatility priors needed, see bvar details. Random Walk (RW) stochastic volatility specification, following priors used $$\begin{aligned}&\sim \mathrm{N}(\theta_A, \Omega_A) \\ \ln \lambda_{,0} &\sim \mathrm{N}(\mu_{\ln \lambda_{,0}}, \sigma^2_{\ln \lambda_{,0}}) \\ \phi_i &\sim \mathrm{IG}(\alpha_{\phi_i},\beta_{\phi_i})\end{aligned}$$ \(\) \(k(k-1)/2\) vector collects free parameters \(\) row-major order, \(\ln \lambda_{,0}\) time \(t=0\) values (initial conditions) \(\ln \lambda_{,t}\). Furthermore, \(\phi_i\) log volatility innovation variances. AR(1) (AR1) stochastic volatility specification, following priors used $$\begin{aligned}&\sim \mathrm{N}(\theta_A, \Omega_A) \\ \gamma_{0} &\sim \mathrm{N}(\theta_{\gamma_0}, \Omega_{\gamma_0}) \\ \gamma_{1} &\sim \mathrm{N}(\theta_{\gamma_1}, \Omega_{\gamma_1}) \\ \ln \lambda_{0} &\sim \mathrm{N}(\theta_{\ln \lambda_{0}}, \Omega_{\ln \lambda_{0}}) \\ \Phi &\sim \mathrm{IW}(V_0,m_0)\end{aligned}$$ \(\) \(k(k-1)/2\) vector collects free parameters \(\) row-major order, \(\ln \lambda_0\) time \(t=0\) values (initial conditions) \(\ln \lambda_{t}\). Furthermore, \(\gamma_{0}\) log volatility intercepts, \(\gamma_{1}\) log volatility slopes, \(\Phi\) log volatility innovation covariance matrix.

title: Specify priors for the steady-state BVAR model — priors
code: #homoscedastic with Jeffreys prior yt <- matrix(rnorm(50), 25, 2)  bvar_obj <- bvar(data = yt)  bvar_obj <- setup(bvar_obj, p=1)  bvar_obj <- priors(bvar_obj,                    lambda_1 = 0.2,                    lambda_2 = 0.5,                    lambda_3 = 1,                    first_own_lag_prior_mean = rep(1,2),                    theta_Psi = rep(0, 2),                    Omega_Psi = diag(0.1, 2, 2),                    Jeffrey = TRUE,                    SV = FALSE,                    SV_type = NULL,                    SV_priors = NULL)                     #RW stochastic volatility yt <- matrix(rnorm(50), 25, 2)  bvar_obj <- bvar(data = yt)  bvar_obj <- setup(bvar_obj, p=1)  k <- bvar_obj$setup$k n_free_params_A <- bvar_obj$setup$n_free_params_A  SV_priors_RW <- list( theta_A             =  rep(0, n_free_params_A), Omega_A             =  diag(1000, n_free_params_A), mu_log_lambda_0     =  rep(0, k), sigma2_log_lambda_0 =  rep(1000, k), alpha_phi           =  rep(5, k), beta_phi            = (rep(5, k) - 1) * rep(0.1, k) )  bvar_obj <- priors(bvar_obj,                    lambda_1 = 0.2,                    lambda_2 = 0.5,                    lambda_3 = 1,                    first_own_lag_prior_mean = rep(1,2),                    theta_Psi = rep(0, 2),                    Omega_Psi = diag(0.1, 2, 2),                    SV = TRUE,                    SV_type = "RW",                    SV_priors = SV_priors_RW)                     #AR1 stochastic volatility yt <- matrix(rnorm(50), 25, 2)  bvar_obj <- bvar(data = yt)  bvar_obj <- setup(bvar_obj, p=1)  k <- bvar_obj$setup$k n_free_params_A <- bvar_obj$setup$n_free_params_A  SV_priors_AR <- list( theta_A            =  rep(0, n_free_params_A), Omega_A            =  diag(1000, n_free_params_A), theta_gamma_0      =  rep(0.1, k), Omega_gamma_0      =  diag(1000, k), theta_gamma_1      =  rep(0.9, k), Omega_gamma_1      =  diag(10, k), theta_log_lambda_0 =  rep(0.1, k)/(1-rep(0.9, k)), Omega_log_lambda_0 =  diag(1000, k), V_0                = (10 - k - 1) * diag(k), m_0                =  10 )  bvar_obj <- priors(bvar_obj,                    lambda_1 = 0.2,                    lambda_2 = 0.5,                    lambda_3 = 1,                    first_own_lag_prior_mean = rep(1,2),                    theta_Psi = rep(0, 2),                    Omega_Psi = diag(0.1, 2, 2),                    SV = TRUE,                    SV_type = "AR1",                    SV_priors = SV_priors_AR)

what: Restrict VAR coefficients to zero — restrict_beta
title: Restrict VAR coefficients to zero — restrict_beta
text: Applies zero restrictions VAR coefficient matrix (beta) setting corresponding prior variances Omega_beta value near zero (prior means zero default Minnesota prior).

title: Restrict VAR coefficients to zero — restrict_beta
code: restrict_beta(x, restriction_matrix)

title: Restrict VAR coefficients to zero — restrict_beta
text: x steady-state bvar object passed priors. restriction_matrix numeric matrix dimension k*p x k entries 0 indicate coefficients restricted zero entries 1 indicate unrestricted coefficients.

title: Restrict VAR coefficients to zero — restrict_beta
text: steady-state bvar object restriction matrix stored setup Omega_beta updated accordingly.

title: Restrict VAR coefficients to zero — restrict_beta
code: yt <- matrix(rnorm(50), 25, 2)  bvar_obj <- bvar(data = yt)  bvar_obj <- setup(bvar_obj, p=2)  bvar_obj <- priors(bvar_obj,                    theta_Psi = rep(0, 2),                    Omega_Psi = diag(0.1, 2, 2))                     p <- bvar_obj$setup$p k <- bvar_obj$setup$k  restriction_matrix <- matrix(1, k*p, k)  restriction_matrix[1, 1] <- 0 restriction_matrix[4, 2] <- 0  bvar_obj <- restrict_beta(bvar_obj, restriction_matrix)

what: Set up the steady-state BVAR model — setup
title: Set up the steady-state BVAR model — setup
text: Prepares components needed prior specification estimation. Also computes OLS estimates.

title: Set up the steady-state BVAR model — setup
code: setup(   x,   p = 1,   deterministic = c("constant", "constant_and_dummy", "constant_and_trend"),   dummy = NULL )

title: Set up the steady-state BVAR model — setup
text: x steady-state bvar object created bvar. p Integer. lag order VAR. Default 1. deterministic Character. deterministic component include. One "constant" (default), "constant_and_dummy", "constant_and_trend". dummy Numeric vector dummy variable. used deterministic = "constant_and_dummy". Default NULL.

title: Set up the steady-state BVAR model — setup
text: steady-state bvar object setup list containing required components prior specification estimation, also OLS estimates.

title: Set up the steady-state BVAR model — setup
code: yt <- matrix(rnorm(50), 25, 2)  bvar_obj <- bvar(data = yt)  bvar_obj <- setup(bvar_obj)

what: Plot stochastic volatility estimates and forecasts — stochastic_volatility_plot
title: Plot stochastic volatility estimates and forecasts — stochastic_volatility_plot
text: Produces time series plots posterior stochastic volatility estimates estimation sample together predictive paths forecast horizon fitted steady-state bvar object stochastic volatility.

title: Plot stochastic volatility estimates and forecasts — stochastic_volatility_plot
code: stochastic_volatility_plot(   x,   ci = 0.95,   vol = "log_lambda",   stat = "mean",   plot_idx = NULL,   xlim = NULL,   ylim = NULL )

title: Plot stochastic volatility estimates and forecasts — stochastic_volatility_plot
text: x steady-state bvar object passed fit stochastic volatility enabled. ci Numeric. Interval level. Default 0.95. vol Character. Volatility representation: "log_lambda" (default) "sd". stat Character. Posterior summary statistic use point estimate: "mean" (default) "median". plot_idx Integer vector. Indices variables plot. NULL (default), variables plotted. xlim Numeric vector length 2. Optional x-axis limits. ylim Numeric vector length 2. Optional y-axis limits.

title: Plot stochastic volatility estimates and forecasts — stochastic_volatility_plot
text: invisible list : estimated: List posterior summaries estimation sample: point: \(T \times k\) matrix posterior point estimates (mean median depending stat) lower: \(T \times k\) matrix lower credible bounds upper: \(T \times k\) matrix upper credible bounds predicted: List posterior summaries forecast horizon: point: \((T+H) \times k\) matrix predictive point estimates (mean median depending stat) lower: \((T+H) \times k\) matrix lower bound prediction interval upper: \((T+H) \times k\) matrix upper bound prediction interval

title: Plot stochastic volatility estimates and forecasts — stochastic_volatility_plot
text: function supports two volatility representations: "log_lambda": log-volatility states "sd": implied standard deviations selected variable, function plots posterior point estimate path credible bands estimation sample, predictive point estimate path prediction intervals forecast horizon.

title: Plot stochastic volatility estimates and forecasts — stochastic_volatility_plot
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ yt <- matrix(rnorm(50), 25, 2)  bvar_obj <- bvar(data = yt)  bvar_obj <- setup(bvar_obj, p = 1, deterministic = "constant")  k <- bvar_obj$setup$k n_free_params_A <- bvar_obj$setup$n_free_params_A  SV_priors <- list( theta_A             =  rep(0, n_free_params_A), Omega_A             =  diag(1000, n_free_params_A), mu_log_lambda_0     =  rep(0, k), sigma2_log_lambda_0 =  rep(1000, k), alpha_phi           =  rep(5, k), beta_phi            = (rep(5, k) - 1) * rep(0.1, k) )  bvar_obj <- priors(bvar_obj,                    theta_Psi = rep(0, 2),                    Omega_Psi = diag(0.1, 2, 2),                    SV = TRUE,                    SV_type = "RW",                    SV_priors = SV_priors)  bvar_obj <- fit(bvar_obj,                 H = 8,                 d_pred = matrix(rep(1, 8)),                 iter = 200,                 warmup = 50,                 chains = 1,                 cores = 1,                 verbose = FALSE,                 auto_write = FALSE)  res <- stochastic_volatility_plot(bvar_obj, ci = 0.95) } # }

what: Summarise a fitted steady-state BVAR model — summary.bvar
title: Summarise a fitted steady-state BVAR model — summary.bvar
text: Computes prints posterior summaries fitted steady-state bvar object. printed output depends whether model homoscedastic includes stochastic volatility (RW AR1 specification).

title: Summarise a fitted steady-state BVAR model — summary.bvar
code: # S3 method for class 'bvar' summary(object, pars = NULL, stat = "mean", t = NULL, ...)

title: Summarise a fitted steady-state BVAR model — summary.bvar
text: object steady-state bvar object passed fit. pars Character vector parameter names include. NULL (default), available parameters displayed. stat Character. Posterior summary statistic display: "mean" (default) "median". t Integer. Time index innovation covariance matrix stochastic volatility estimated. NULL, latest available t used. ... Additional arguments (currently unused).

title: Summarise a fitted steady-state BVAR model — summary.bvar
text: Returns input object invisibly.

title: Summarise a fitted steady-state BVAR model — summary.bvar
text: function summarises following estimated parameters: beta: kp×k VAR coefficient matrix Psi: k×q steady-state parameter matrix Sigma_u: innovation covariance matrix (k×k homoscedastic, T×k×k stochastic volatility) Random Walk stochastic volatility: : k×k lower triangular matrix ones diagonal describes contemporaneous interaction endogenous variables phi: k-dimensional vector log volatility innovation variances AR1 stochastic volatility: : k×k lower triangular matrix ones diagonal describes contemporaneous interaction endogenous variables gamma_0: k-dimensional vector log volatility intercepts gamma_1: k-dimensional vector log volatility slopes Phi: k×k log volatility innovation covariance matrix

title: Summarise a fitted steady-state BVAR model — summary.bvar
code: if (FALSE) { # \dontrun{ yt <- matrix(rnorm(50), 25, 2) bvar_obj <- bvar(data = yt) bvar_obj <- setup(bvar_obj, p = 1, deterministic = "constant") bvar_obj <- priors(bvar_obj,                    theta_Psi = rep(0, 2),                    Omega_Psi = diag(0.1, 2, 2))  bvar_obj <- fit(bvar_obj,                 H = 1,                 d_pred = matrix(1),                 iter = 200,                 warmup = 50,                 chains = 1,                 cores = 1,                 verbose = FALSE,                 auto_write = FALSE)  summary(bvar_obj) } # }

title: SteadyStateBVAR 0.0.0.9000
text: Initial development version. Preparing first CRAN submission.
